title: Tutorial: Planning a Student Diet Survey

turns.speaker: Dr Sato
turns.text: So, your project is on what students actually eat during exam periods. Interesting topic. How are you planning to collect the data?

turns.speaker: Ellie
turns.text: Our original plan was a questionnaire asking people to remember everything they'd eaten over the past week. But we've gone off that idea.

turns.speaker: Raj
turns.text: Yes, because people are terrible at remembering. They forget snacks, they underestimate portions, and some of them, frankly, lie to look healthier than they are.

turns.speaker: Dr Sato
turns.text: You're quite right — recall over a whole week is unreliable. So what's your alternative?

turns.speaker: Ellie
turns.text: A food diary. Participants record each meal at the moment they eat it, on their phone. That way there's no relying on memory days later.

turns.speaker: Dr Sato
turns.text: Much better. Real-time recording cuts the recall problem sharply. But it introduces a different risk — can you see what it is?

turns.speaker: Raj
turns.text: I think so. If people know we're watching what they eat, they might start eating more healthily than usual — just because they're being observed.

turns.speaker: Dr Sato
turns.text: Exactly, that's the observer effect. There's no perfect fix, but a longer study helps — after a few days people forget to perform and slip back to normal habits. Now, disagreement time: how long should the diary run?

turns.speaker: Ellie
turns.text: I'd say two weeks. That's long enough for people to relax, but short enough that they don't give up.

turns.speaker: Raj
turns.text: I'd actually argue for just one week. Two weeks is a lot to ask of a busy student in exam season — I think we'd lose too many participants.

turns.speaker: Dr Sato
turns.text: Both of you have a point, but on balance I side with Ellie. The observer effect really does need those extra days to settle. Go with two weeks, but keep the daily entry very quick so people don't drop out.

turns.speaker: Raj
turns.text: Fair enough. Two weeks, but make it painless. I can accept that.

turns.speaker: Dr Sato
turns.text: Good. Now let's divide the work. Someone builds the phone form, someone recruits participants, and I'll sort out the nutritional database you'll need to code the meals.

turns.speaker: Raj
turns.text: I'll build the phone form. I've done a bit of app design, so that's the natural job for me.

turns.speaker: Ellie
turns.text: And I'll recruit the participants. I know lots of people across different courses, so I can get a varied sample.

turns.speaker: Dr Sato
turns.text: Perfect. I'll handle the nutritional database. One rule, though — aim for at least fifty participants, or your results won't mean much. Have the recruitment done by the end of week four.

turns.speaker: Ellie
turns.text: Fifty participants by week four. Understood. Thanks — I'm glad we ditched the memory questionnaire, it really would have let us down.

questionGroups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.
questionGroups.instructions_html: Why did the students abandon their original questionnaire?

options.text: People remember a whole week's food poorly.
Questionnaires take too long to write.

questionGroups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.
questionGroups.instructions_html: What new risk does the food-diary method introduce?

options.text: Participants may eat more healthily because they are observed.
Phones may run out of battery.

questionGroups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.
questionGroups.instructions_html: How is the disagreement about the diary's length resolved?

options.text: The tutor supports Ellie's two weeks.
They agree on Raj's one week.
